title: SQLi to Full Admin Takeover via Credential Extraction + Forged JWT
attack_narrative: Step 1: Attacker exploits the UNION SQLi in /rest/products/search (INJ-001) to extract all rows from Users table including MD5 password hashes and email addresses. Step 2: MD5 hashes are trivially cracked (AUTH-002/CRYPTO-001) but cracking is unnecessary. Step 3: The hardcoded RSA private key in insecurity.ts (AUTH-003/CRYPTO-002) allows direct JWT forgery. Step 4: Attacker crafts JWT with payload {data: {id: 1, email: 'admin@juice-sh.op', role: 'admin'}} signed with the known private key using RS256. Step 5: Attacker uses forged JWT to access all admin endpoints, view all user data, modify products, and access accounting functions. The SQLi provides exact user IDs and roles needed; the hardcoded key eliminates password cracking. Zero-to-full-admin with no authentication.
exact_code:
models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%'...`)
jwt.sign(user, privateKey, { expiresIn: '6h', algorithm: 'RS256' })
impact: Complete application takeover. Attacker gains admin access, exfiltrates all user data (emails, addresses, payment cards, order history), modifies product catalog, and impersonates any user from an unauthenticated starting position.
fix_recommendation: Parameterize all SQL queries. Remove hardcoded private key and load from secrets manager. Replace MD5 with bcrypt. Each fix independently breaks this chain.

title: XXE File Read + Hardcoded Private Key + JWT Forgery (No Auth Required)
attack_narrative: Step 1: Attacker uploads crafted XML to POST /file-upload (no auth). XML contains entity referencing file:///proc/self/cwd/lib/insecurity.ts. With noent:true, libxmljs2 resolves the entity. Step 2: HTTP 410 error response includes up to 400 chars of file content via utils.trunc(xmlString, 400), exposing the RSA private key. Step 3: Alternatively, GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub (unauthenticated) confirms the key pair. Step 4: With private key extracted via XXE, attacker forges admin JWT. This chain works entirely through file upload with no SQL injection or login needed.
exact_code:
vm.runInContext('libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true })', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })
next(new Error('B2B customer complaints... ' + utils.trunc(xmlString, 400) + ' (' + file.originalname + ')'))
impact: Full authentication bypass via JWT forgery. XXE provides independent path to extract private key even if source repository is private.
fix_recommendation: Disable external entity resolution (noent: false). Add authentication to file upload. Remove /encryptionkeys public endpoint. Externalize private key.

title: XSS Cookie Theft via Stored XSS + Missing HttpOnly + JSONP + Fields Leak
attack_narrative: Step 1: Attacker exploits sanitize-html 1.4.2 bypass (COMP-003) to inject stored XSS via product review. Step 2: No global CSP (MISCONFIG-006) allows script execution. Step 3: JWT cookie has no HttpOnly flag (AUTH-006), so document.cookie is readable. Step 4: XSS payload calls fetch('/rest/user/whoami?callback=exfil&fields=password,totpSecret') - JSONP endpoint (AUTH-014) wraps response including password hash and TOTP secret (DATA-003) in attacker callback. Step 5: Attacker receives session token, password hash, and TOTP secret, completely defeating both password auth and 2FA.
impact: Complete account takeover of any user who views the XSS-injected page. Session token, password hash, and TOTP secret exfiltrated in single exploit - full 2FA bypass.
fix_recommendation: Set httpOnly:true on auth cookies. Remove JSONP support. Add strict global CSP. Upgrade sanitize-html. Add field allowlist to /whoami.

title: Basket Manipulation + Coupon Forgery for Financial Fraud
attack_narrative: Step 1: Attacker authenticates (basket ID 2). Step 2: POST /api/BasketItems with duplicate JSON keys: {ProductId:1, BasketId:2, BasketId:5, quantity:99}. Ownership check validates basketIds[0]=2 (matches bid), but item inserted into basket 5 (victim) using basketIds[last]. Step 3: IDOR on GET /rest/basket/5 verifies items added. Step 4: Attacker knows z85 coupon encoding from insecurity.ts and generates valid coupon: z85.encode('APR26-99') for 99% discount. Step 5: Applies forged coupon to victim basket. Victim checks out manipulated basket.
impact: Financial fraud via price manipulation. Attacker adds items to any basket and applies forged maximum-discount coupons.
fix_recommendation: Reject duplicate JSON keys. Validate BasketId matches authenticated user after resolution. Use HMAC signatures for coupons.

title: Self-Registration as Admin + CSRF Password Change Lockout
attack_narrative: Step 1: Register admin account via POST /api/Users with role:admin - registerAdminChallenge middleware only logs, does not strip role (AC-009). Step 2: Log in with new admin account. Step 3: Wildcard CORS (AC-003) allows cross-origin requests. Step 4: Craft page that triggers GET /rest/user/change-password?new=hacked&repeat=hacked on victim admin's browser (AUTH-007 - GET-based password change). Step 5: currentPassword check is conditional: if not provided, it is skipped entirely. Legitimate admin's password changed without knowledge. Step 6: Attacker controls own admin account AND has locked out legitimate admin.
impact: Complete admin account takeover. Attacker creates own admin account then locks out legitimate admins via CSRF password change.
fix_recommendation: Strip role from registration body. Make currentPassword mandatory. Convert password change to POST. Add CSRF tokens. Restrict CORS.

title: LFI via Data Erasure Layout + Error Handler Stack Trace Leak
attack_narrative: Step 1: Authenticated attacker POSTs to /dataerasure with layout:/etc/passwd. Blocklist only checks for ftp/ctf.key/encryptionkeys - /etc/passwd passes. Step 2: Handlebars renders /etc/passwd as template. If parsing fails, error.message includes file content. Step 3: errorhandler middleware returns full error with stack trace. Step 4: For renderable files, first 100 chars returned directly via html.slice(0, 100). Step 5: By iterating paths, attacker reads arbitrary files 100 bytes at a time.
impact: Arbitrary file read from server filesystem. Configuration files, source code, environment variables, and database credentials exposed.
fix_recommendation: Replace blocklist with explicit allowlist. Never spread req.body into template context. Disable errorhandler in production.

title: Direct eval() RCE via Username SSTI in User Profile Rendering
attack_narrative: Step 1: Attacker sets username to pug interpolation pattern matching /#{(.*)}/, e.g., #{global.process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('id').toString()}. Step 2: When usernameXssChallenge is enabled, code extracts content between #{ and } and passes it to eval(). Step 3: eval() runs arbitrary JavaScript with full Node.js process access including require(), process, fs. Step 4: The evaluated result is then injected into the pug template and compiled, creating a DOUBLE injection vector. Neither the Sonnet nor blind Opus pass explicitly identified the eval() as an independent RCE vector beyond challenge tracking.
impact: Full Remote Code Execution. Arbitrary OS commands, file read/write, database exfiltration, backdoor installation, lateral movement.
fix_recommendation: Remove eval() entirely. Use a sandboxed evaluator with no global scope access if template interpolation is needed for the challenge.

title: CSP Header Injection via User-Controlled profileImage URL
attack_narrative: Step 1: Profile page constructs CSP by interpolating profileImage directly into the header value: img-src 'self' ${user?.profileImage}; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval'. Step 2: Attacker sets profileImage to 'https://evil.com; script-src * unsafe-inline'. Step 3: Resulting CSP injects additional directives. The challenge code confirms exploitability - it checks for script-src followed by unsafe-inline in the profileImage field. Step 4: Attacker weakens CSP on their own profile page, enabling arbitrary script loading from external origins.
impact: CSP bypass on profile page. Arbitrary CSP directive injection weakens or eliminates the only page with CSP protection.
fix_recommendation: Never interpolate user values into CSP headers. Use nonce-based CSP. Validate profileImage against strict URL pattern.

title: Data Export IDOR via req.body.UserId for Memory Retrieval
attack_narrative: Step 1: dataExport uses loggedInUser from Authorization header for email-based queries but uses req.body.UserId for memory queries. Step 2: While appendUserId() middleware normally overwrites req.body.UserId, the design is fundamentally flawed - memory queries should use loggedInUser.data.id directly. Step 3: If appendUserId fails in its try/catch, the attacker-supplied UserId from the POST body survives and is used for MemoryModel.findAll.
exact_code:
const loggedInUser = security.authenticatedUsers.get(req.headers?.authorization?.replace('Bearer ', ''))
memories = await MemoryModel.findAll({ where: { UserId: req.body.UserId } })
impact: Potential exposure of other users' memory/photo uploads via the data export endpoint by manipulating UserId.
fix_recommendation: Use loggedInUser.data.id directly for all queries. Never trust req.body.UserId for authorization-critical queries.

title: Unauthenticated Product Review Creation via Unprotected PUT Endpoint
attack_narrative: Step 1: PUT /rest/products/:id/reviews has NO security.isAuthorized() middleware in server.ts (line 2958). Step 2: PATCH (update) and POST (like) require isAuthorized(), but PUT (create) is unprotected. Step 3: Unauthenticated attacker creates reviews with arbitrary author fields since author is taken from request body, not JWT. Step 4: Enables fake reviews, reputation manipulation, and potential stored XSS via review content.
exact_code:
app.get('/rest/products/:id/reviews', showProductReviews())
app.put('/rest/products/:id/reviews', createProductReviews())  // NO isAuthorized()
app.patch('/rest/products/reviews', security.isAuthorized(), updateProductReviews())
app.post('/rest/products/reviews', security.isAuthorized(), likeProductReviews())
impact: Unauthenticated creation of product reviews with forged author names. Reputation attacks, phishing, and stored XSS vector.
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() to PUT /rest/products/:id/reviews. Derive author from JWT, not request body.

title: Rate Limit on Password Reset Fully Bypassed via X-Forwarded-For Rotation
attack_narrative: Verification confirms bypass is trivially exploitable. Rate limiter at line 2671 uses keyGenerator that reads X-Forwarded-For header. app.enable('trust proxy') at line 2667 means Express trusts this header. Attacker rotates X-Forwarded-For values (1.1.1.1, 1.1.1.2, etc.), each getting a fresh rate limit bucket. The 100-per-5-min limit becomes unlimited. Combined with low-entropy security answers and the unauthenticated email enumeration via /rest/user/security-question, this enables brute-force account takeover.
impact: Complete bypass of rate limiting. Unlimited brute-force of security answers for any known email address.
fix_recommendation: Use req.ip only. Configure trust proxy for specific proxy IPs. Add account lockout after N failed attempts.

title: Open Redirect Allowlist Bypass Confirmed via includes() Substring Matching
attack_narrative: Confirmed: isRedirectAllowed uses url.includes(allowedUrl) for each allowlist entry. Attacker constructs: GET /redirect?to=https://evil.com/phish?ref=https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop - passes because the full URL contains the allowlisted URL as a substring. The isUnintendedRedirect function uses startsWith (correct) but is only used for challenge detection, NOT access control. The redirect is issued regardless of isUnintendedRedirect result.
impact: Arbitrary redirect to attacker-controlled domains for phishing. Can chain with OAuth flows.
fix_recommendation: Parse URLs with new URL() constructor. Compare hostname strictly against allowlist of permitted hostnames.

title: ZIP Path Traversal Guard Allows Writes Anywhere Within Application Directory
attack_narrative: Sonnet noted the guard but the bypass is worse than described. The check absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.')) passes for ANY traversal within the project tree. path.resolve('uploads/complaints/../../ftp/legal.md') resolves to /app/ftp/legal.md which contains /app (the cwd). The WriteStream opens at the traversed path. ANY file in the application directory is writable: routes/*.ts, config files, the SQLite database, encryption keys. Combined with ts-node-dev in production deps, overwritten .ts files could be auto-compiled.
impact: Arbitrary file write within application directory. Can overwrite source code, config, database, or keys.
fix_recommendation: Use path.basename() to strip directory components. Validate resolved path starts with extraction directory plus path.sep.

title: denyAll() Middleware Secret Predictable via Math.random() PRNG State Recovery
attack_narrative: denyAll() protects PUT/DELETE Users, DELETE Products, and other critical routes using expressJwt with secret = '' + Math.random(). V8 xorshift128+ has 128 bits of state. If attacker observes two sequential Math.random() outputs (via timing or other exposed values), they reconstruct PRNG state and predict the secret. The secret is generated once at require-time and never rotates. Once known, attacker forges JWTs to access user modification and deletion endpoints.
impact: Bypass of all denyAll-protected routes including user modification/deletion if PRNG state is recovered.
fix_recommendation: Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'). Better: implement denyAll as unconditional 403 middleware.
